title: Number of Islands
description: Given a 2D grid of '1's (land) and '0's (water), count the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.
hints: When you find a '1', how do you know which other '1's belong to the same island?
Use BFS or DFS from each unvisited '1' to mark all connected land as visited. Each new BFS/DFS start = new island.
Iterate through grid. When you find '1', increment count and BFS/DFS to mark all connected '1's as '0' or visited.
follow_ups: Can you solve it using Union-Find?
What if the grid is very large and distributed?

title: Clone Graph
description: Given a reference of a node in a connected undirected graph, return a deep copy of the graph. Each node contains a value and a list of its neighbors.
hints: The tricky part is handling nodes that appear multiple times due to cycles. How do you avoid duplicating them?
Use a hash map to track original node -> cloned node. If you've already cloned a node, reuse it.
BFS or DFS. When visiting a node, create its clone and store in map. For each neighbor, clone if not in map, then add to clone's neighbors.
follow_ups: What if the graph is directed?
How would you handle a weighted graph?

title: Course Schedule
description: There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to numCourses-1. You are given prerequisites pairs where [a, b] means you must take b before a. Determine if you can finish all courses (i.e., no circular dependency).
hints: This is about detecting if a directed graph has a cycle. What algorithm detects cycles in directed graphs?
Topological sort — if you can produce a valid ordering, there's no cycle.
Use Kahn's algorithm: compute in-degrees, start BFS from nodes with in-degree 0. If all nodes are processed, no cycle exists.
follow_ups: Can you return the actual course order?
What if some courses can be taken simultaneously?

title: Word Ladder
description: Given two words beginWord and endWord, and a dictionary wordList, return the length of the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord, changing one letter at a time. Each transformed word must exist in the wordList.
hints: You're looking for the shortest path between two nodes. What algorithm finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs?
BFS gives you shortest path. Each word is a node, edges connect words that differ by one letter.
BFS from beginWord. For each word, try changing each character to a-z. If the new word is in wordList and unvisited, add to queue.
follow_ups: Can you find all shortest transformation sequences?
How would you optimize with bidirectional BFS?

title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path
description: Given a weighted directed graph with n nodes and a source node, find the shortest path from the source to all other nodes. Edge weights are non-negative.
hints: A greedy approach works here — always process the closest unvisited node. What data structure efficiently gives you the minimum?
A min-heap (priority queue) lets you extract the closest node in O(log n).
Initialize distances to infinity except source=0. Use min-heap. Pop minimum, relax all neighbors. Skip if already found shorter path.
follow_ups: What if edges can have negative weights?
How would you reconstruct the actual path?
